title: CNC Machine Shop Business
metaTitle: How to Start a CNC Machine Shop: Real Costs, Earnings, and Steps
metaDescription: Honest guide to starting a CNC machine shop business: real startup costs, CAD/CAM skill, machine capital, realistic earnings, B2B contracts, and what gets shops in trouble.
bestFor: Experienced machinists or engineers with CAD/CAM skills and access to capital who want a B2B manufacturing business
biggestRisk: Sinking heavy capital into machines that sit idle because the shop has no steady stream of profitable, repeatable work to fill them
whatItIs: A CNC machine shop makes precision metal and plastic parts for other businesses using computer-controlled mills, lathes, and routers. Work ranges from low-volume prototypes and one-off repair parts to repeating production runs for manufacturers, and customers come from aerospace, medical, automotive, defense, industrial equipment, and small hardware startups. It is fundamentally a B2B manufacturing business: you turn a CAD drawing into a programmed toolpath (CAM), set up the machine and fixturing, cut the part to tight tolerances, and inspect it before shipping. The barriers to entry are real, serious machine capital, deep CAD/CAM and machining knowledge, and the discipline to quote, schedule, and hold tolerances profitably, which is also what protects margins for shops that run well.
dailyReality: A typical day blends programming, setup, and machine tending. You read prints and model parts in CAD/CAM, generate and simulate toolpaths, set up workholding and tools, run first articles, then inspect with calipers, micrometers, gauges, or a CMM before producing the run. Around the spindle time you are quoting jobs (a major skill in itself), ordering material and tooling, deburring and finishing parts, and managing delivery deadlines. Especially when starting solo, you wear every hat, salesperson, programmer, operator, and quality inspector, and a single mis-set offset or wrong tolerance can scrap expensive material and blow a deadline.

startupCostsItemized.item: CNC mill and/or lathe (used entry-level to production-grade)
Measuring and inspection equipment (calipers, micrometers, gauges, optional CMM)
Shop space lease, three-phase power, and buildout/deposits
Air compressor, coolant, material handling, and shop infrastructure
General liability + property insurance and business registration/LLC
Initial raw material stock (aluminum, steel, plastics)

realEarnings.yearOne: Most new shops reinvest heavily and earn little net profit in year one while paying down machines and building a customer base; owner take-home often runs $0 to $5,000 per month and is frequently negative early. Operators who start with a captive customer or contract in hand do meaningfully better.
realEarnings.experienced: An established one-to-three-person shop with steady repeat customers and disciplined quoting commonly nets the owner $5,000 to $30,000 per month, depending on machine utilization, mix, and how much production (versus low-margin one-offs) it runs. Revenue is far higher than take-home, since material, tooling, machine payments, and labor consume most of it.
realEarnings.topEarners: Shops with multiple machines, lights-out or multi-shift production, skilled programmers, and long-term contracts (especially regulated aerospace/medical work under AS9100 or ISO 13485) gross hundreds of thousands to millions per year and can be very valuable to sell. Getting there takes years of reinvestment, certifications, employees, and consistent quality.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: Machine utilization (idle machines lose money fast), quoting accuracy, the mix of repeatable production versus one-off prototypes, material and tooling discipline, and landing reliable repeat B2B customers. Industry certifications unlock higher-paying regulated work.
realEarnings.perHour: Shops bill machine time, commonly $60 to $150+ per spindle hour depending on the machine and tolerances. The owner's effective hourly take, counting quoting, programming, setup, and admin, is far lower early on and improves as utilization and repeat work grow.

howToStart: Months 1-3 (before buying anything): Validate demand. The most common shop failure is buying machines with no work to fill them. Line up at least one anchor customer or a contract, ideally from your existing network in a manufacturing-heavy region, before committing capital.

Month 1: Get honest about skills. You need real machining experience plus CAD/CAM proficiency (Fusion 360 is the affordable entry point; Mastercam/SolidWorks are industry standards). If you lack hands-on machining time, get it first, this is not a learn-as-you-go business with expensive machines.

Months 2-4: Acquire equipment deliberately. Many shops start with one good used VMC (vertical machining center) or lathe rather than overbuying. Secure shop space with adequate power (often three-phase), set up the LLC, and get general liability and property insurance.

Months 3-6: Take your first jobs, quoting carefully and tracking real cost per part. Build a quality process, first-article inspection, measurement, and documentation, because reliability is what earns repeat B2B work.

Months 6-18: Pursue repeatable production work and, if targeting aerospace or medical, begin the path to ISO 9001 / AS9100 / ISO 13485 certification, which unlocks higher-margin regulated contracts. Reinvest profit into utilization (a second machine, automation) only as steady demand justifies it.

mustHave: Real machining experience, speeds and feeds, tolerances, workholding, and how parts actually cut
CAD/CAM proficiency to model parts and generate reliable, efficient toolpaths
Print reading and GD&T (geometric dimensioning and tolerancing) literacy
Quoting and basic shop math to price jobs profitably, not just competitively
canLearn: Specific machine controls and post-processors (Haas, Fanuc, etc.)
separators: Accurate quoting and high machine utilization, the difference between a profitable and a struggling shop
Landing repeatable production contracts instead of living on low-margin one-offs
Industry certifications (AS9100, ISO 13485) and a reputation for on-time, in-tolerance delivery

whatPeopleGetWrong: Buying machines before securing work, then bleeding cash on payments while the spindles sit idle
Underquoting jobs, forgetting to account for programming, setup, fixturing, material, tooling wear, and scrap
Underestimating how much capital tooling, fixturing, and material tie up beyond the machine itself
Taking only one-off prototype work, which is hard to schedule and rarely as profitable as repeat production
Neglecting a real quality/inspection process, so out-of-tolerance parts cause rejects, returns, and lost customers
Overbuying capability (five-axis, big production cells) before having the demand to keep it utilized

toolsAndEquipment.item: CNC vertical machining center (mill) and/or CNC lathe
toolsAndEquipment.note: The core asset. A solid used machine beats overextending on new capacity you cannot fill.
Fusion 360 is the affordable starting point; Mastercam/SolidWorks are industry standards for complex work.
An ongoing consumable cost that adds up fast; quality tooling pays for itself in finish and tool life.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Workholding and fixturing (vises, chucks, soft jaws, fixtures)
toolsAndEquipment.note: Repeatable, rigid workholding is essential for tolerances and efficient setups.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Inspection equipment (calipers, micrometers, gauges, optional CMM)
toolsAndEquipment.note: You cannot sell precision parts you cannot verify. A CMM unlocks tighter, regulated work.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Air compressor, coolant system, and material handling
toolsAndEquipment.note: Shop infrastructure machines depend on; size it to your equipment.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Shop space with adequate (often three-phase) power
toolsAndEquipment.note: Power, floor loading, and ventilation requirements rule out many cheap spaces.

methods: Direct outreach to local manufacturers, OEMs, and engineering firms needing parts or overflow capacity
Online manufacturing marketplaces and instant-quote platforms (Xometry, Fictiv, Protolabs network, MFG.com)
Referrals from suppliers, other shops passing overflow, and your existing industry network
A professional website showcasing capabilities, tolerances, certifications, and lead times
Industry certifications (ISO 9001, AS9100, ISO 13485) that get you onto approved-vendor lists for regulated buyers
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Other businesses, manufacturers, equipment makers, aerospace and medical companies, defense suppliers, and hardware startups, concentrated in manufacturing regions. The best early customers are companies already in your professional network who trust your work.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: Building a reliable repeat customer base typically takes one to two years. B2B buyers are cautious and reward proven reliability, so the first jobs are auditions; consistent on-time, in-tolerance delivery is what converts them into steady accounts.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Consumer-style advertising and broad social media. This is a relationship- and reliability-driven B2B business; direct outreach, marketplaces, referrals, and certifications matter far more than ads.

howItScales.toFullTime: Yes, but it is capital- and time-intensive to get there. Reaching a strong full-time income usually requires high machine utilization, repeat production work, and often a second machine or shift, it is rarely a quick or part-time path.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: Yes, and this is how shops grow. Hiring skilled machinists and programmers, then implementing standardized processes and quality systems, lets the owner move from running machines to running the business. Lights-out or multi-shift production is a common scaling lever.
howItScales.sellable: Highly. Established shops with machines, repeat contracts, certifications, trained staff, and documented quality systems are attractive acquisitions and sell for solid multiples. Tangible equipment plus contracted recurring work make them more sellable than most service businesses.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: Capital for additional machines and automation, skilled programmers and machinists, robust quality systems and certifications, accurate quoting at volume, and a sales effort to keep growing capacity utilized.

strongFit: You have real machining experience and solid CAD/CAM skills
You have access to capital and can survive a slow, reinvestment-heavy ramp
You can sell and quote B2B work and hold tight tolerances reliably
You are in or near a manufacturing region with industrial demand
poorFit: You want a low-capital, fast-income business
You have no machining or CAD/CAM background
You dislike quoting, scheduling, and detailed quality documentation
You cannot tolerate heavy upfront investment with little early profit
askYourself: Do I have committed work or an anchor customer before I buy a single machine?
Can I quote accurately enough to make money, not just win jobs on price?
Do I have the capital and patience to keep machines utilized through a one-to-two-year ramp?

faqs.question: How much does it really cost to start a CNC machine shop?
faqs.answer: Realistically $40,000 on the very low end with a single good used machine, modest tooling, and a small space, up to $350,000 or more for a production-grade setup. Remember the machine is only part of it, tooling, workholding, inspection gear, software, material, power, and space all add up, and machines tie up capital long before they produce profit.

faqs.question: What skills do I need before starting?
faqs.answer: You need genuine machining experience (understanding tolerances, speeds and feeds, workholding) plus CAD/CAM proficiency to program parts, and print-reading with GD&T literacy. Quoting accurately is its own critical skill. This is not a business to learn from scratch on expensive equipment; most successful owners come from years as machinists or manufacturing engineers.

faqs.question: Do I need certifications?
faqs.answer: Not to start with general commercial work, but certifications gate the higher-margin jobs. ISO 9001 is broadly useful, AS9100 is typically required for aerospace, and ISO 13485 for medical devices. Earning them takes time and documentation but gets you onto approved-vendor lists for buyers who pay well for proven quality.

faqs.question: Why do so many machine shops struggle financially?
faqs.answer: The most common reason is buying machines before having steady work, so expensive equipment sits idle while payments come due. Underquoting is the second, forgetting to price programming, setup, tooling, and scrap. Shops that validate demand first and quote disciplined, profitable jobs are the ones that survive.

faqs.question: Should I take one-off prototypes or production runs?
faqs.answer: Both have a place, but repeatable production work is generally more profitable and easier to schedule because the programming and setup are amortized over many parts. One-off prototypes pay per setup and can be lucrative but are unpredictable. A healthy shop usually blends some prototype work with anchor production contracts.

faqs.question: Can I start in a home garage?
faqs.answer: Small benchtop machines can run in a garage for hobby or very light work, but a real commercial shop usually needs adequate (often three-phase) power, floor loading for heavy machines, ventilation, and zoning that permits manufacturing. Most serious shops lease industrial space; check power and zoning before committing to any location.

faqs.question: How long until the business is profitable?
faqs.answer: Plan on a slow ramp. Many shops reinvest most revenue into machines, tooling, and material in the first year or two, with the owner taking little home early. Profitability comes as machine utilization rises and repeat B2B customers stabilize, typically over one to two years of disciplined operation.

dataSources: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Machinists and Tool and Die Makers occupational employment and wage data
Modern Machine Shop and industry trade publications (shop economics, machine utilization, billing rates)
CNC machine and software vendor pricing (Haas, Tormach, Autodesk Fusion 360, Mastercam) for capital and software costs
Manufacturing marketplaces (Xometry, Fictiv) and approved-vendor/certification requirements (ISO 9001, AS9100, ISO 13485)
Machinist and shop-owner communities (Practical Machinist) for real-world startup and earnings patterns
